APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper is squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP venues: it studies evaluation metrics for data-driven NLG, compares 21 automatic metrics (including novel grammar-based ones), and proposes a new combined metric (RAINBOW) with strong sentence-level correlation (up to ρ=0.81) (Sections 4,7, Conclusion). It targets the right audience by addressing core evaluation practices used in NLP/NLG (BLEU/ROUGE/CIDEr/METEOR and human Likert judgments, ICC, statistical tests) and evaluates on multiple standard datasets and systems (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL; RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) (Sections 3,5, Tables 7–13). The problem formulation, experimental methodology and error analysis (system- and dataset-specific results, binning of ratings, referenceless/grammar-based metrics) follow community standards and are well positioned within related work (Section 10), making it a strong fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper offers notable novelty: it is (as claimed) the first to apply grammar-based metrics (GBMs) to sentence-level NLG evaluation (see Section 4.2) and compares a large set (21) of automatic metrics across three end-to-end data-driven systems and three datasets. It also proposes a new composite metric, RAINBOW, reporting much higher sentence-level correlation with human judgements (up to ρ=0.81) than existing metrics (max ρ≈0.33), and provides a detailed error analysis of scale- and data-specific failures. While largely an empirical and evaluative contribution rather than a new modeling paradigm, these elements represent a clear and informative advance over prior work.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a logical structure (Intro, Datasets, Metrics, Human Data, Error Analysis, Related Work, Appendix) and clearly lists main contributions early with bullet points, which aids comprehension. However the provided text contains many duplications, OCR/LaTeX artifacts and misplaced line numbers (e.g. repeated paragraphs, broken sentences, and interleaved table fragments) that disrupt readability. Tables and examples appear informative when legible, but several are poorly formatted here, and there are occasional grammatical/repetition issues that require cleanup.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares a wide set of relevant baselines: three representative end-to-end NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and 21 automated metrics (WBMs and novel GBMs) across three datasets/two domains (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) as stated in the Introduction and Datasets/4 Metrics sections. Comparisons appear fair and reproducible (they use the systems’ outputs as provided by authors, make code/data public) and employ significance testing (Williams’ test, Wilcoxon) and detailed error analysis (Sections 7–8, Table 6 and Appendix) while explicitly discussing prior conflicting findings (Related Work) and how RAINBOW differs and outperforms existing metrics.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses a widely-acknowledged, important problem (heavy reliance on automatic metrics in NLG) and provides a comprehensive empirical study (21 metrics including novel grammar-based measures, 3 systems, multiple datasets) that convincingly demonstrates limits of current practice (metrics unreliable at sentence-level and for medium/good outputs). Its proposal of the RAINBOW metric—with reported sentence-level correlation up to ρ=0.81 versus ρ≈0.33 for existing metrics—plus detailed error analysis and public release of code/data, is likely to change evaluation practices and spur follow-up work on combined/referenceless metrics. While not guaranteed to be transformative immediately, the work has strong potential to be widely built upon across NLG and related fields.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments are broad and detailed: the paper evaluates 21 automatic metrics (including novel grammar-based ones) across three end-to-end NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), with human evaluation (6‑point Likert, 3 raters, ICC=0.45) and extensive per-dataset/system analyses and error analyses (e.g. bins, MR types) supporting the main claims. Related work is discussed across NLG, MT and image captioning and limitations are acknowledged (metric failures on mid/good outputs, system- and data-specificity, parser caveats). However, the paper (in the provided text) lacks ablation/sensitivity analyses for the proposed RAINBOW metric or its components and gives limited implementation/hyperparameter detail in the excerpt (though the authors state code/data will be released), which hampers full reproducibility and deeper understanding of what drives RAINBOW’s performance.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper makes clear empirical claims and reports substantial experiments (three systems, three datasets, 21 metrics) with human annotation procedures (Section 5: 3 raters per output, ICC=0.45) and many correlation results and significance tests (Tables 9–14, use of Williams’ and Wilcoxon tests). The experimental methodology is generally sound (appropriate baselines, multiple datasets, per-system and per-dataset analyses), but important methodological/details are missing in the provided excerpt: the proposed RAINBOW metric is claimed to reach ρ=0.81 at sentence level yet no formulation, training/validation procedure or supporting table/plot for that result is included here, and there is no discussion of multiple-comparison correction. These omissions create a nontrivial gap between the strong claim for RAINBOW and the presented evidence, so while most analyses seem correct, the unsupported/high-impact claim lowers the technical confidence.
